statement.markdown: Given the root of a binary tree, return the inorder traversal of its nodes' values. (i.e., from left to right, visit the left subtree, the current node, and then the right subtree). Implement an iterative solution to perform the inorder traversal.
statement.inputFormat: The first line contains the number of nodes in the tree. The next lines contain the node values, with -1 representing a null node. The nodes are given in a pre-order traversal format (root, left, right).
statement.outputFormat: The inorder traversal of the tree's nodes' values, separated by spaces.

constraints.description: The number of nodes in the tree.
The values of the nodes in the tree.

examples.explanation: The inorder traversal of the given tree is [2, 1, 4, 3, 5].
The inorder traversal of the given tree is [1, 2, 3].

hints.text: To solve this problem iteratively, think about how you can simulate the recursive call stack using a data structure of your choice.
Consider using a stack to keep track of nodes that you need to visit. Inorder traversal visits the left subtree, then the current node, and finally the right subtree.

To ensure you visit nodes in the correct order, think about how you can use the stack to temporarily store nodes as you traverse down the left subtree, and then pop them off to visit the node and its right subtree.

editorial.title: Iterative Inorder Traversal of a Binary Tree
editorial.intuition: The problem requires traversing a binary tree in an inorder manner (left-root-right) without using recursion. The key observation is that a stack can be used to mimic the recursive call stack, allowing for an iterative approach.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force approach would involve using recursion to perform the inorder traversal. However, this is not allowed as per the problem statement. The recursive approach would have a time complexity of O(n) and a space complexity of O(h), where h is the height of the tree, which can be n in the worst case.
editorial.betterSolution: The better solution involves using a stack to store nodes. We start by pushing the root node and all its left children onto the stack. Then, we pop a node from the stack, add its value to the result, and push its right child and all its left children onto the stack. This process continues until the stack is empty.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution has a time complexity of O(n) and a space complexity of O(n), where n is the number of nodes in the tree. This is because we visit each node once and store at most n nodes in the stack.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the optimal solution can be proved by showing that it visits nodes in the correct order (inorder). We start by pushing the root node and all its left children onto the stack. When we pop a node from the stack, we add its value to the result. Then, we push its right child and all its left children onto the stack. This ensures that we visit the left subtree, the current node, and then the right subtree, which is the definition of inorder traversal.
editorial.dryRun: A dry run of the algorithm with a sample tree can help verify its correctness. For example, with a tree having nodes 4, 2, 1, 3, 6, 5, 7, the algorithm will correctly output 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7.
commonMistakes: Not handling the case when the current node is None correctly.
Not pushing the right child onto the stack after popping a node.
Not adding the value of the popped node to the result.
Not checking if the stack is empty before popping a node.
Using a queue instead of a stack, which would result in a preorder traversal.
Not considering the case when the tree is not balanced.
